steps.title: Identify the appositive structure
steps.content: The blank introduces a noun ("advocate") that is part of the subject. This noun is immediately followed by a proper noun ("Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez") that identifies the person—an appositive structure.

steps.title: Apply the restrictive appositive rule
steps.content:
Rule: a restrictive appositive — one that identifies which person is meant — takes no commas. "Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez" tells us which lawyer and cycling advocate; without the name, the subject is unidentified. The name is essential.

A: "...cycling advocate Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez, has identified..." — trailing comma only; unmatched and incorrectly inserts a boundary before the verb. Incorrect.

B: "...cycling advocate, Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez, has identified..." — commas on both sides signal a nonrestrictive appositive, implying the name is optional extra information. It is not. Incorrect.

C: "...cycling advocate Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez has identified..." — no commas; the name directly and necessarily identifies the subject. Correct.

D: "...cycling advocate, Ernesto Hernandez-Lopez has identified..." — opening comma only; unmatched and signals a nonrestrictive open without a proper close. Incorrect.
